journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Assessment of gelatin-epigallocatechin gallate nanoparticles with hyaluronic acid coating for treating dry eye associated keratopathy in rats

contrib-group.aff: 1)Institute of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, School of Veterinary Medicine, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan
2)Department of Ophthalmology, National Taiwan University Veterinary Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
3)Graduate Institute of Biomedical Materials and Tissue Engineering, College of Biomedical Engineering, Taipei Medical University, Taipei, Taiwan
4)Graduate Institute of Molecular and Comparative Pathobiology, School of Veterinary Medicine, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Lin C-T: eyevet.lin@gmail.com, Institute of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, School of Veterinary Medicine, College of Bio resources and Agriculture, National Taiwan University, No. 1, Section 4, Roosevelt Road, Taipei 106, Taiwan

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2025 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p:
This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0:
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: Keratoconjunctivitis sicca (KCS) is a common ocular disease in dogs characterized by inflammation of the cornea and conjunctiva. This study investigates the effectiveness of gelatin-epigallocatechin gallate nanoparticles with hyaluronic acid coating (GEH NPs) and various artificial tears in treating KCS led keratopathy in a rat model. Eighteen female Sprague-Dawley rats, aged 6 to 8 weeks were randomly assigned to 6 groups of 3. All rats except the Sham group underwent unilateral exorbital and infraorbital lacrimal gland excisions to induce KCS. The treatment groups included a KCS group without treatment, a GEH group treated with GEH NPs, and 3 groups receiving different artificial tears. Clinical examinations, including slit-lamp biomicroscopy, optical coherence tomography (OCT), and ocular surface analyser-VET (OSA-VET) were conducted preoperatively at 1 and 2 weeks postoperatively. For treating KCS associated keratopathy, the GEH NPs showed superior outcomes and consistently ranked among the strongest treatment options in most aspects evaluated, including reduced corneal irregularity, improved tear film stability, and lower fluorescein scores, indicating better corneal integrity. Additionally, the GEH group displayed the least inflammatory cell infiltration and maintained a healthier epithelial structure, further underscoring its protective effects. GEH was administered twice daily while other treatment groups were administrated 3 times daily, highlighting its efficacy even with a lower dosing frequency. The artificial tears demonstrated variable benefits on KCS led keratopathy across different evaluations. GEH NPs exhibited excellent protective effects and therapeutic potential on dry eye associated keratopathy based on this study.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Keratoconjunctivitis sicca (KCS), commonly known as dry eye, is a prevalent condition in veterinary ophthalmology, especially in dogs, with reported prevalence ranging from 4% to 35% [19, 37]. Artificial tears are frequently used to treat KCS, in which, hyaluronic acid (HA) application in artificial tears has increased due to its benefits. HA binds closely to cells on the corneal surface [13], has higher residence time [29, 36], spreads evenly on the cornea [30] and improves epithelial cell migration [15]. Research in human and veterinary medicine showed hyaluronic acid-containing artificial tears outperformed to those with hydroxypropylmethylcellulose and carbomer [18, 36].

Despite the convenience of traditional eye drops for veterinary patients and their owners, there are some notable limitations. These include limited drug retention time and nasolacrimal duct drainage of the drug [11], resulting in less than 5% of the drug retaining on the ocular surface [6, 35]. A potential solution is the use of nanoparticles (NPs) as a colloidal drug-delivery system. NPs can encapsulate and enhance intraocular uptake of the drug, control drug release rate, target delivery site and achieve higher drug retention rate, thereby reducing dosing frequency [1, 2, 7, 10, 11, 33]. Nanoparticle size smaller than 800 nm can be readily absorbed by cornea and conjunctiva via endocytosis or phagocytosis [10, 14, 34]. In this study, a biopolymer, gelatin (a denatured form of collagen) was selected as the raw component for NPs preparation due to the fact that collagen is the main constitute of corneal stroma, and can also interact well with corneal and conjunctival glycoproteins [4, 16].

This study also incorporates epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), a polyphenol found in tea, known for its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. EGCG has demonstrated effectiveness in suppressing inflammatory response in human corneal epithelial cells in vitro experiment [5] and murine in vivo experiment [22]. However, EGCG, as an antioxidant, is prone to oxidation, which can reduce its bioactivity during drug delivery. Therefore, encapsulation of EGCG in NPs can protect it from oxidation and enhance its active effect after transportation. Furthermore, the advantages of using nanoparticles as eye drops include enhanced accumulation of nanoparticles on the cornea and conjunctiva, improved drug bioavailability in ocular tissues, and a sustained drug release rate that prolongs therapeutic effects while reducing dosing frequency [6, 11, 14, 17]. It is proven that gelatin-epigallocatechin gallate nanoparticles with a hyaluronic acid coating (GEH NPs) can help drug retention on the ocular surface, and effectively relieve the dry eye syndrome in a benzalkonium chloride (BAC)-induced rabbit model [17].

In this study, we aim to confirm if GEH NPs can effectively treat KCS led keratopathy in a rat model. Specifically, we evaluated the therapeutic effects of GEH NPs in comparison to existing treatments with 3 commercial artificial tears. Additionally, the effectiveness of GEH NPs on the KCS led keratopathy was assessed by Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) and Ocular Surface Analyzer-VET (OSA-VET) throughout the experiment.

sec.title: Production of nanoparticles
sec.p:
The GEH NPs were produced following the protocol by Huang et al., 2018 [17] (Fig. 1Fig. 1.
Schematic illustration of gelatin-epigallocatechin gallate nanoparticles with hyaluronic acid coating (GEH NPs) synthesization, and their application in keratoconjunctivitis sicca (KCS) treatment. (A) GEH NPs were fabricated by mixing an equal amount of gelatin and Epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) to form nanoparticles. Subsequently, hyaluronate (HA) solution was added to coat on the surface to from GEH NPs. (B) Treatment of KCS rats with GEH NPs eye drops. Adjusted GEH NPs at a concentration containing 20 μg/mL of EGCG were applied twice daily topically to rats underwent unilateral lacrimal gland (LG) excision.) under dim light conditions. Type A gelatin powder (bloom 110 from porcine skin, Product Number G6144; Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was dissolved in deionized water at 40°C to obtain a 0.42% (w/v) solution. EGCG (≥95%, Product Number 324880; Sigma-Aldrich) was dissolved in deionized water to achieve a concentration of 4.44 mg/mL. Sodium hyaluronate 1% (ArtiAid®; Maxigen Biotech Inc., New Taipei City, Taiwan) was diluted to 0.125% with deionized water.

In a clean glass container, 450 μL of Type A gelatin solution was mixed with 450 μL of EGCG solution (4.44 mg/mL) at 990 rpm for 5 min to form the gelatin-EGCG (GE) nanoparticles. Subsequently, 100 μL of 0.125% of sodium hyaluronate solution was added, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 10 min to obtain 1 mL of GEH NPs. The size and the zeta potential which represents the charge of NPs were examined after by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK) via dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis, with 3 measurements taken per batch at 25°C. For therapeutic testing in the animal study, the eye drops containing GEH NPs were diluted with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) in a final EGCG concentration of 20 μg/mL. All products were stored at 4°C under dark conditions. The morphology of nanoparticles was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; HT7700, Hitachi, Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), where a diluted GEH NP solution was placed on a nickel mesh and dried for examination.

sec.title: Animals
sec.p: Eighteen Sprague-Dawley female rats aged 6 to 8 weeks were used for this study. All animal care and experimental methods adhered to the ARVO Statement for the Use of Animals in Ophthalmic and Vision Research. The protocol was approved by the National Taiwan University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC Approval No. NTU-110-EL-00086). Rats were housed under a 12-hr light/12-hr dark cycle, normal temperature and humidity, and food and water ad libitum.

The rats were randomly separated into 6 groups of 3. The Sham group (n=3) did not undergo unilateral lacrimal glands (LG) excision. The remaining groups underwent unilateral gland excision to induce KCS: KCS group (n=3), GEH group (n=3), I-drop group (n=3), OPT group (n=3) and Refresh group (n=3). The KCS group received no treatment post-surgery, while the GEH group had GEH NPs applied twice daily to the surgical side of the eye starting one day after surgery. I-drop group, OPT group and Refresh group had I-DROP® VET PLUS (Lot: 6N44B; I-Med Animal Health, Saint-Laurent, Quebec, Canada), Optixcare® Eyelube gel (Lot: 13453; CLC MEDICA, Ontario, Canada) and Refresh Plus™ (Lot: E92721; Allergan Ltd, North Chicago, IL, USA) applied respectively on the surgical side of eye 3 times per day since 1 day after surgery. Detailed ingredients of the artificial tears used in this study are shown in the Table 1Table 1.Ingredients of tested agents used in this study
Tested agentsIngredients
I-DROP® VET PLUSWater for injection, sodium chloride, 0.25% hyaluronan, glycerin, sodium phosphate, potassium sorbate, EDTA. Dosing frequency: 3 times/day.
Optixcare® Eyelube gelDeionized water, sorbitol, carbomer, 0.25% hyaluron, sodium hydroxide, disodium EDTA, Cetrimide. Dosing frequency: 3 times/day.
Refresh Plus™0.5% carboxymethylcellulose sodium, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, potassium chloride, purified water, sodium chloride, sodium lactate. Dosing frequency: 3 times/day.
GEH NPsGelatin, epigallocatechin gallate EGCG (20 mg/mL), hyaluronic acid (HA, 0.000125%), phosphate buffer saline (PBS). Dosing frequency: 2 times/day.
*Underlined are the active ingredients of the artificial tears..

sec.title: Establishment of an induced KCS rat model
sec.p: Rats were anesthetized using a combination of Tiletamine hydrochloride and Zolazepam hydrochloride (Zoletil®; containing 250 mg Tiletamine as HCl and 250 mg Zolazepam as HCl in 5 mL, Virbac AH, Inc., Carros, France) at 30 mg/kg, along with Xylazine hydrochloride at 10 mg/kg, administered intraperitoneally. All ophthalmic examinations were conducted prior to surgery on Day 0. Other than the Sham group, unilateral excision of left exorbital and intraorbital lacrimal glands (ELG and ILG) was performed while the right eye remained intact. For the Sham group individuals, incisions were made to expose left ILG and ELG without excision.

Prior to surgery, the rats received oral meloxicam 2 mg/kg (Mobic® Tablets 7.5 mg; Boehringer Ingelheim Ellas A.E., Koropi, Greece) and oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (Amoxicillin capsules 500 mg; China Chemical & Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Taiwan) at least 1 hr before the surgery. All surgeries were conducted by the same investigator, involving an incision lateral to the lateral canthus to access and excise the ILG using ophthalmic forceps and tenotomy scissors. The incision then extended caudally towards the cranioventral part of the ear to access and excise the ELG. Surgical incisions were closed using 5–0 Nylon sutures. Post-operative oral medication included oral meloxicam (1 mg/kg, once a day for 2 days, starting from Day 1) and amoxicillin (25 mg/kg, twice daily for 5 days, starting after rat’s recovery from anesthesia).

sec.title: Clinical ophthalmic examinations
sec.p: Clinical ophthalmic evaluations of the rats were conducted through a series of ophthalmic and laboratory tests on Day 0 (pre-surgery), Day 7, and Day 14 post-surgery. On each examination day, all ophthalmic examinations were performed in the same order, that was, assessing palpebral opening, Schirmer’s tear test (STT), OSA-VET, fluorescein staining, slit-lamp biomicroscopy, and OCT. An additional STT was also performed on Day 1 prior to any treatment to confirm successful KCS model establishment. Among examinations, fluorescein staining and OCT evaluations were performed under anesthesia with 30 mg/kg Zoletil and 10 mg/kg Xylazine injected intraperitoneally.

Palpebral openings (both height and width) were measured with an electronic caliper (Mitutoyo, Kawasaki, Japan) (Fig. 2Fig. 2.
Images of clinical examination and evaluation. (A) Palpebral opening measurements, with X and Y representing the width and height, respectively. (B) Ocular Surface Analyzer-VET (OSA-VET) device containing a real time camera was placed in front of the eye, projecting the multi-ring image. Images were captured through the device. (C) Fluorescein stain scoring. 0.1 mL of 1 mg/mL sodium fluorescein was applied on the cornea for 2 min, rinsed with normal saline, then scored with a slit-lamp biomicroscope. The cornea was divided into 3 portions and scored based on density of fluorescein.). The size of the STT strips was used according to a previous study [21]. STT paper strips (Omni Schirmer; Omni Lens Pvt. Ltd., Gujarat, India) were cut to 1.0 × 15 mm, placed in the lower lateral third of the conjunctival fornix for 1 min. The strips were then removed and the length of wetted strips were recorded. The measurements were conducted by the same investigator to eliminate variations between different operators.

Fluorescein staining involved applying 0.1 mL of 1 mg/mL sodium fluorescein (Omni Fluro; Omni Lens Pvt. Ltd.) on the cornea for 2 min, then rinsing with normal saline. Fluorescein stains were assessed through a slit-lamp biomicroscope (SL-17; KOWA, Tokyo, Japan) and scored with a method modified from a previous study [31], where the cornea was divided into dorsal, intermediate and ventral sections and given a score of 0–3 for each section. Scoring from 0–3 was based on the density of the fluorescein: 0=no fluorescein uptake;1=sparse dots of fluorescein uptake; 2=dense dots of fluorescein uptake; 3=very dense dots or areas with fluorescein uptake. Scores between 0 and 1 were calculated as 0.5, between 1 and 2 were calculated as 1.5, between 2 and 3 were calculated as 2.5. The total sum of scores of 3 sections (highest possible score of 9) was calculated (Fig. 2C). Images were captured by digital camera (Pentax Q, Pentax Ricoh Imaging Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and a yellow filter.

OSA-VET (SBM Sistemi, Torino, Italy) was used to assess corneal irregularity and non-invasive tear film break-up time (NIBUT). Corneal irregularity was scored on a scale of 0–5 based on the distortion of a projected white ring, as mentioned in a previous study [39]. (0: no distortion; 1: distortion in 1 quadrant; 2: distortion in 2 quadrants; 3: distortion in 3 quadrants; 4: distortion in all quadrants; 5: A ring could not be projected on the cornea due to severe distortion). Smooth corneas result in no distortion of the white ring, whereas increased corneal surface irregularity leads to greater distortion. NIBUT measured the time until the first sign of distortion in a multiple-ring image projected on the cornea. Three measurements were averaged for analysis.

During OCT examination, rats were secured on a platform under general anesthesia. OCT Spectralis® (Heidelberg Engineering Inc., Franklin, MA, USA) with anterior segment module was used to scan the entire cornea of the left eye systemically from left to right with a standardized scanning pattern of the same interval. The computer screen of the OCT displayed the corresponding position of the scan on the cornea, allowing for precise localization of the imaging data. Only median plane images were used for comparison.

sec.title: Histopathological examination
sec.p: After clinical evaluations on Day 14, rats were euthanized using a combination of Zoletil® and Xylazine for anesthesia, followed by intracardial administration of Potassium Chloride 2 mmol/kg (KCL Inj. 15%; Taiwan Biotech Co., Ltd., Taiwan). All 18 globes and conjunctiva were fixed in Modified Davidson’s solution. After dehydration, the specimens were fixed in paraffin, cross-sectioned and stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) stain. Inflammatory cell presence in the corneal stroma was evaluated under high power field (HPF) microscopy. The evaluation involved averaging counts from 5 random locations on the cornea, with the following classification of cell infiltration as advised by a veterinary pathologist: none −0 inflammatory cells/HPF, rare −less than or equal to 5 inflammatory cells/HPF, mild −6 to 10 inflammatory cells/HPF, moderate −10–20 inflammatory cells/HPF, severe−more than 20 inflammatory cells/HPF. Conjunctival assessments focused on the fornices and palpebral conjunctiva, measuring epithelial thickness (μm) and goblet cell density (number of goblet cell clusters/400 μm of conjunctival lining) in 3 areas each eye under high magnification (400x), modified from a previous study [21]. The respective averages were analyzed and compared.

sec.title: Data interpretation and statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analyses were performed using Prism 9 for macOS version 9.3.1 (GraphPad Software Inc., La Jolla, CA, USA). The results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) in graphs. Data were analyzed by two-way mixed ANOVA for all clinical ophthalmic examinations and one-way ANOVA for histopathological examinations, followed by Tukey’s test for post-hoc comparisons. Results were considered statistically significant at P<0.05.

sec.title: Characterization of nanoparticles
sec.p: As illustrated in Fig. 1, gelatin and EGCG solutions were mixed to form nanoparticles, which were then decorated with HA. A milky-white colloidal solution of GEH NPs was fabricated at final EGCG concentration at 2.0 mg/mL and HA at 0.0125%. For animal testing, this GEH NPs solution was diluted to a final EGCG concentration of 20 μg/mL for use as eyedrops.

DLS of particle size, zeta (ζ)-potential pattern, 3 values of GEH NPs, and TEM image were analyzed (Fig. 3Fig. 3.
Characterization of gelatin-epigallocatechin gallate nanoparticles with hyaluronic acid coating (GEH NPs). Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) results of (A) particle size, (B) zeta (ζ)-potential pattern, (C) 3 values of GEH NPs, and (D) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. It showed a single peak of narrow size and charge distribution of the prepared GEH NPs (A, B). The value of particle size of GEH NPs was 270.1 ± 1.88 nm (C). With a very small size, GEH NPs can facilitate its endocytosis by corneal and conjunctival cells to work intracellularly [17]. The ζ-potential of the particles was −21.6 ± 0.5 mV which was consistent with a previous study of the same protocol [17], indicating successful production of GEH. A low polydispersity index (PdI) of the GEH NPs at 0.24 ± 0.03 (C) in a mono-dispersed colloidal solution made it remain stable effectiveness without aggregation or precipitation for a long time. The TEM image revealed that the GEH NPs were round and spherical in shape, demonstrating a good suspension without precipitation or aggregation (D, scale bar: 1 μm).). It showed a single peak of narrow size and charge distribution of the prepared GEH NPs (Fig. 3A, 3B). The value of particle size of GEH NPs was 270.1 ± 1.88 nm (Fig. 3C). With a very small size, GEH NPs can facilitate its endocytosis by corneal and conjunctival cells to work intracellularly [17]. The ζ-potential of the particles was −21.6 ± 0.5 mV which was consistent with a previous study of the same protocol [17], indicating successful production of GEH. The GEH NPs exhibited a low polydispersity index (PdI) of 0.24 ± 0.03 (Fig. 3C), indicating a monodispersed colloidal solution that remains stable and effective without aggregation or precipitation over time. The TEM image revealed that the GEH NPs were round and spherical in shape, demonstrating a good suspension without precipitation or aggregation (Fig. 3D, scale bar: 1 μm).

sec.title: Clinical ophthalmic examinations
sec.p:
Palpebral opening: As the widths (X of Fig. 2A) of palpebral openings were measured equally in all individuals at all time points, only the heights (Y of Fig. 2A) of palpebral openings were used for comparison. No significant differences were observed among the groups on Day 0. On Day 7, while the KCS group showed significantly lower palpebral opening values compared to the Sham group (P<0.05), all treatment groups (the GEH, I-drop, OPT and refresh groups) maintained relatively higher values, indicating better ocular comfort than the KCS group (Fig. 4AFig. 4.
Examinations of palpebral opening, Schirmer’s tear test (STT) and non-invasive tear film break-up time (NIBUT). (A) Palpebral opening measurements across different groups at various time points. *P<0.05. The KCS group had significantly smaller palpebral openings than all other groups on Days 7 and 14. Although not statistically significant, trend of larger palpebral opening of all treatment groups is noted compared to the KCS group. (B) STT values of the different groups at various time points. *P<0.05, **P<0.01. The Sham group had significantly higher STT values than all other groups at all time points. On Day 14, the KCS group (valued 0 mm) had significantly lower STT values than the GEH, I-drop, and OPT groups. (C) NIBUT of the different groups at various time points. *P<0.05, **P<0.01, ***P<0.001. In NIBUT measurements, the Sham group exhibited significantly longer time at all time points. On Day 7, the GEH group had significantly longer NIBUT than the KCS group.). By Day 14, although both the KCS and I-Drop groups had smaller palpebral openings compared to the Sham group (P<0.05), the treatment groups demonstrated a trend toward larger palpebral openings, suggesting more favorable comfort in comparison to the KCS group, despite no significant differences being noted (Fig. 4A).

Schirmer’s tear test (STT): On Day 0, no significant differences in STT values were noted among groups. However, on Day 1, Day 7 and Day 14, the Sham group consistently showed higher STT values than all other groups, indicating normal tear secretion. In contrast, on Day 1, all groups with LG excision showed tear shortage following establishment of the KCS model than the Sham group (P<0.05 compared with GEH group and P<0.01 compared with KCS, I-drop, OPT and Refresh group). In comparison, on Day 7, all treatment groups exhibited higher STT values compared to the KCS group, suggesting a positive trend in tear quantity, even though these differences were not statistically significant. On Day 14, the KCS group had undetectable STT values, measuring 0 mm. In contrast, the GEH, I-Drop, and OPT groups demonstrated significantly higher STT values than the KCS group, indicating their effectiveness in promoting tear quantity (P<0.05) (Fig. 4B).

sec.title: OSA-VET evaluations−NIBUT and corneal irregularity
sec.p: For NIBUT, no significant differences were observed on Day 0. By Day 7, all groups that underwent LG excision had significantly shorter NIBUT times compared to the Sham group (P<0.05 compared with KCS, GEH, OPT and Refresh groups, P<0.001 compared with I-drop group). Notably, the GEH group demonstrated significantly longer NIBUT compared to the KCS group (P<0.05), indicating that GEH effectively enhances tear film stability in the LG excision groups. On Day 14, although all LG excision groups continued to show shorter NIBUT times than the Sham group (P<0.05 compared with KCS group, P<0.01 compared with GEH, I-drop, OPT and Refresh groups), the GEH, I-drop and Refresh groups exhibited trends toward improved NIBUT, suggesting their potential benefits in maintaining tear stability post-surgery (Fig. 4C).

Corneal irregularity scoring was used to evaluate the smooth level of corneal surface. On Day 0, no significant differences in irregularity scores were noted among groups. On Day 7, although not statistically significant, the GEH, I-drop and Refresh group had lower irregularity scores compared to the KCS group (Fig. 5AFig. 5.
Examination of corneal irregularity. (A) Corneal irregularity scores for different groups at various time points. *P<0.05. Scoring of corneal irregularity: 0- no distortion; 1- distortion in 1 quadrant; 2- distortion in 2 quadrants; 3- distortion in 3 quadrants; 4- distortion in all quadrants; 5- A ring could not be projected on the cornea due to severe distortion. On Day 7, the GEH, I-drop and Refresh groups had lower corneal irregularity scores compared to the KCS group. On Day 14, much lower corneal irregularity scores were noted in the GEH group than all other treatment groups, indicating less corneal damage and a smoother corneal surface in the GEH group. (B) Sham group on Day 14 (score 0) showing no distortion of the ring, indicating smooth corneal surface. (C) GEH group on Day 14 (score 1) with slight distortion in the left lower quadrant. (D) Refresh group on Day 14 (score 4) with distortion in all quadrants. (E) KCS group on Day 14 (score 5) with no distinct projection of the ring on the cornea, indicating rough and irregular corneal surface.), revealing a less extent of corneal damage. On Day 14, the GEH group revealed the lowest irregularity scores among all treatment groups, indicating the least corneal damage and a smoother ocular surface. The round globe shape with smooth corneal surface was observed in the GEH treated eyes (Fig. 5B).

sec.title: OCT evaluations
sec.p:
The quantification results of OCT assessments for corneal stromal and corneal epithelial thickness are shown in Fig. 6.Fig. 6.
Corneal thickness assessment by Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT). (A) Measurements of corneal stromal thickness indicated the GEH group with the closest stromal thickness to the Sham group among all groups with the least corneal edema. In contrast, the KCS and I-Drop groups had much thicker stromal thickness than other groups. (B) Measurements of corneal epithelial thickness indicated the GEH group with the least thickness among treatment groups despite no significant difference between groups. (C-H) OCT images and measurements of epithelial and stromal thickness at Day 14−C: Sham group; D: KCS group; E: GEH group; F: I-drop group; G: OPT group; H: Refresh group. Increased corneal opacity was noted in all groups following LG excision, with much thicker corneal stroma in the KCS and I-drop groups. Measurements of corneal stromal thickness indicated the GEH group with the closest stromal thickness to the Sham group among all groups with the least corneal edema. In contrast, the KCS and I-Drop groups had much thicker stromal thickness than other groups (Fig. 6A). Measurements of corneal epithelial thickness indicated the GEH group with the least thickness among treatment groups, despite no significant difference between groups (Fig. 6B). OCT images and measurements of epithelial and stromal thickness on Day 14 are presented in Fig. 6 (Fig. 6C–H, C: Sham group; D: KCS group; E: GEH group; F: I-drop group; G: OPT group; H: Refresh group). Increased corneal opacity was noted in all groups with LG excision, with much thicker corneal stroma and edema in the KCS and I-drop groups.

sec.title: Fluorescein scores of corneas
sec.p:
A lower fluorescein score (FS) indicates a more intact corneal epithelium. The KCS group had the highest FS among all groups, indicating more severe damage following LG excision in the untreated KCS group (Fig. 7AFig. 7.
Fluorescein scores (FS) of corneas. FS of cornea were analyzed in all groups. The KCS group had the highest FS among all groups. Scoring from 0-3 was based on the density of the fluorescein: 0=no fluorescein uptake;1=sparse dots of fluorescein uptake; 2=dense dots of fluorescein uptake; 3=very dense dots or areas with fluorescein uptake. Scores between 0 and 1 were calculated as 0.5, between 1 and 2 were calculated as 1.5, between 2 and 3 were calculated as 2.5. The total sum of scores of 3 sections (highest possible score of 9) was calculated. On Day 7, the GEH group had the lowest FS among the KCS and other treatment groups. On Day 14, the GEH and OPT groups had significantly lower FS than the KCS and other treatment groups. (*P<0.05, **P<0.01). (B–G) Images of fluorescein staining on the corneas. B: Sham group, score 0 (Day 14); C: KCS group, score 7 with large areas stained with green fluorescein dye (Day 14); D: GEH group, score 1.5 (Day 140; E: I-drop group, score 3 (Day 14); F: OPT group, score 2 (Day 14); G: Refresh group, score 3 (Day 14).). On Day 7, the GEH group had the lowest FS among the KCS and other treatment groups. On Day 14, the GEH and OPT groups had significantly lower FS than the KCS group (P<0.05, P<0.01 respectively). Images of fluorescein staining on the corneas on Day 14 are displayed in Fig. 7 (Fig. 7B–G, B: Sham group, score 0; C: KCS group, score 7 with large areas stained with green fluorescein dye; D: GEH group, score 1.5; E: I-drop group, score 3; F: OPT group, score 2; G: Refresh group, score 3).

sec.title: Histopathological examinations
sec.p:
Histopathological analysis of the cornea across different groups on Day 14 is summarized in Table 2Table 2.Summary of histopathological findings of the corneas of different groups on Day 14
GroupCorneal structureLayers of corneal epitheliumInfiltration of inflammatory cells
Sham groupWell-organized and compact6–7None
KCS groupLess organized, collagen layers were loose, sponge-like, epithelial damage.4Moderate
GEH groupEpithelium and stroma were organized.5–6Rare
I-drop groupEpithelium less organized, looser collagen layers.4–5Mild
OPT groupEpithelium and stroma were organized.5–6Mild
Refresh groupEpithelium and stroma were organized.4–5Mild. The KCS group exhibited loss of epithelial layers, moderate infiltration of neutrophils and lymphocytes in the stroma, evidence of epithelial damage with fibrous membrane underneath the damage, and less organized corneal structure (Fig. 8GFig. 8.
Corneal conditions on Day 14. (A–F) H&E-stained cornea from different groups in the axial portion of the median plane (400× magnification). A: Sham group; B: KCS group; C: GEH group; D: I-drop group; E: OPT group with a part of palpebral conjunctiva; F: Refresh group. The KCS group exhibited the most pronounced infiltration of neutrophils and lymphocytes (arrowheads in B), while other treatment groups showed milder infiltration (arrowheads in C–F), particularly the GEH group, which had the least inflammatory cells. Epithelial loss was significant in the KCS group, while other groups displayed minimal to no epithelial loss. (G) H&E-stained cornea of KCS group on Day 14 (100× magnification) showing epithelial damage (arrow) with fibrous membrane beneath, and moderate inflammatory cell infiltration (arrowheads). and Fig. 8B). All artificial tears groups (I-drop group, OPT group and Refresh group) showed mild infiltration of inflammatory cells, with epithelial layers ranging from 4 to 6 (Fig. 8D, 8E, 8F). In particular, the I-drop group displayed less organized corneal structures compared to other artificial tears groups (Fig. 8D). In contrast, the GEH group had the least inflammatory cell infiltration in the stroma and retained 5–6 layers of epithelium (Fig. 8C).

For conjunctival assessments, the KCS group had significantly thinner conjunctival epithelium compared to the Sham, GEH, OPT and Refresh groups on Day 14 (P<0.01 compared with Sham and Refresh groups, P<0.05 compared with GEH and OPT groups) (Fig. 9AFig. 9.
Conjunctival conditions on Day 14. (A) Conjunctival epithelial thickness measurements on Day 14. *P<0.05, **P<0.01. The KCS group exhibited significantly thinner conjunctival epithelium compared to the GEH and OPT groups (P<0.01). (B) Goblet cell density on Day 14. *P<0.05. The KCS group had significantly lower goblet cell density than the GEH and OPT groups (P<0.05). (C-H) H&E-stained conjunctiva images from different groups on Day 14 (400× magnification). C: Sham group; D: KCS group; E: GEH group; F: I-drop group; G: OPT group; H: Refresh group. Arrowheads indicate the location of goblet cells on conjunctiva. Arrow in D indicates the disrupted and thinner epithelial lining in the KCS group.). Goblet cell density also differed significantly, with the KCS group showing lower density than the GEH and OPT groups (Fig. 9B, P<0.05). Under microscopic observation, it was also evident that the conjunctival epithelium of the KCS group had a greater proportion of epithelial damage, characterized by discontinuity of the epithelial lining compared to the Sham group and other treatment groups (Fig. 9C–H).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The interaction and roles of the components in GEH NPs are complex. On the ocular surface, cornea and conjunctiva are negatively charged. The cationic nanoparticles (NPs) can be attracted to the cornea and retained on the cornea for a longer time to prolong or enhance therapeutic effects, thus Gelatin (Type A), a positively charged amino acid was used in producing nanoparticles in this experiment. Gelatin also attracts the negatively charged component (EGCG) to form self-assembled nanoparticles (GE) through electrostatic interactions. Additionally, a coating of HA was applied on its surface to improve mucus-adhesion of NPs with the tear film. The EGCG also plays the role as the anti-inflammatory agent to treat inflammation condition caused by KCS. According to our previous study, the GEH NPs at EGCG concentration of 20 μg/mL can effectively treat KCS in a rabbit model twice daily as eyedrops [17]. Therefore, the same therapeutic dosage and frequency were adopted for treating KCS led keratopathy in the rats in this study.

Keratoconjunctivitis sicca models have been established through various methods and primarily classified into tear production insufficiency, increased tear evaporation, or a combination of both. Based on recent studies [24, 31, 32] and the absence of surgical complications, the excision of ELG and ILG was chosen to effectively mimic keratopathy led by severe aqueous deficiency. The use of female rats was justified, as they tend to exhibit more pronounced keratopathy both clinically and histopathologically, as mentioned in previous studies.

Successful imaging techniques, including OCT and OSA-VET, were utilized in this experiment. While OCT has been well-documented in anterior segment imaging of laboratory animals, studies of OSA-VET evaluations in rats are limited to date. This study was able to assess corneal irregularity assessment and non-invasive break-up time (NIBUT) in minimally restrained rats with OSA-VET. OSA-VET was an ophthalmic equipment modified for veterinary use. However, several limitations arose due to the small size and unique anatomy of rat eyes, which made imaging more challenging compared to larger veterinary patients. For instance, meibography, which is straightforward in larger animals, was difficult to perform on rats due to limited eyelid inversion and globe protrusion. Additionally, tear meniscus height evaluation and lipid layer analysis were difficult due to the albino characteristics of Sprague-Dawley rats. Despite these challenges, OSA-VET’s ease of use under minimal restraint suggests its potential for broader applications in clinical settings.

Palpebral opening measurements have been employed to evaluate ocular pain in previous studies [9, 12, 25]. Although metrics such as blinking rate are often used to evaluate ocular comfort in animals, the squinty behavior of the rats post-surgery made blinking assessment impractical. Therefore, palpebral opening was adopted in this study. Throughout the experiment, only the GEH group and Refresh group exhibited no significant differences in palpebral opening when compared to the Sham group, indicating much better ocular comfort in these groups. While not statistically significant, all treatment groups showed a trend toward larger palpebral openings on Day 7 and Day 14.

Various methods have been used to measure tear volume in rodent KCS models, including the use of phenol red thread [24, 31]. This study adopted a modified version of STT strips (1 × 17 mm), referring to a previous study [21], which were well tolerated by the rats. In this experiment, all groups that underwent LG excision exhibited significantly lower STT values compared to the Sham group on Day 7 and Day 14. In particular, the GEH, I-drop and OPT groups produced significantly higher STT values when compared to the KCS group on Day 14. The presence of hyaluronic acid (HA) in these treatments suggests enhanced tear retention on the ocular surface. When the therapeutic dosage of GEH NPs was diluted to EGCG concentration of 20 μg/mL, the final HA concentration was lowered to 0.000125% in the GEH, which is much lower than all the active ingredients of the tested artificial tears shown in Table 1. The synergistic effect of the nanoparticle combined with EGCG and HA can help to increase the drug retention on ocular surface, inhibiting the inflammation in the KCS eyes as proved in our previous study [17]. The combination of GEH NPs and EGCG, which supports tear retention and reduces inflammation, demonstrates the efficacy of GEH NPs in treating KCS in rat eyes in this study.

Tear film gradually becomes unstable after a blink and the time to tear film instability depends on the composition and dynamics of the tear film. NIBUT, which measures either the time from a blink to tear film discontinuity or the urge for the tested subject to blink, showed that all groups with lacrimal glands excisions had significantly shorter tear film break-up time than the Sham group on Day 7 and Day 14. The GEH group demonstrated a short-term advantage in tear film stability on Day 7, while the I-Drop, OPT, and Refresh groups also showed promising trends, indicating potential benefits in maintaining tear film integrity.

In assessing corneal irregularity, the OSA-VET projected a ring-shaped light onto the cornea, where less distortion indicated a smoother surface. Similar analysis was performed in previous studies on dry eye mice [20, 39]. Only the GEH and Refresh groups maintained low corneal irregularity scores comparable to the Sham group, suggesting effective preservation of smooth and complete ocular surface during damages caused by KCS.

OCT evaluations did not reveal significant differences in corneal stromal and epithelial thickness among groups, though a trend of thicker stromal thickness was noted in the KCS and I-drop groups, indicative of more severe structural changes. These findings were corroborated by histopathological examinations, which showed more pronounced inflammation in these groups. However, due to small sample sizes, further studies are required to determine if there are statistical differences in corneal thickness among groups.

Fluorescein staining, indicating the presence of corneal epithelium damage [3, 27, 38], showed that the KCS and I-drop groups had significantly higher FS than the Sham group on Day 7. While on Day 14, the KCS, I-drop and refresh groups continued to show elevated FS. The GEH and OPT groups, however, had significantly lower FS compared to the KCS group, demonstrating superior protective effects on corneal epithelial integrity by GEH and OPT. Previous studies showed the presence of hyaluronic acid may enhance wound healing [23]. Although the hyaluronic acid-dominant I-drop group did not show a significant advantage in FS compared to the KCS group, a more favorable outcome was observed when hyaluronic acid was combined with carbomer in Optixcare® Eyelube gel (OPT group). Furthermore, the slow release of EGCG from GEH NPs has been shown to inhibit corneal inflammation and promote the repair of damaged epithelial cells, as noted in a previous study [17]. This is consistent with the significantly lower FS in GEH group observed in our experiment. Remarkably, even at a low concentration of EGCG, GEH demonstrated a notable protective effect.

Histopathological analysis revealed reduced epithelial layers and significant inflammatory cell infiltration in the KCS group, consistent with previous studies showing similar findings in KCS rat models [24, 31, 32]. Inflammatory cell infiltration disrupts the organization of corneal cells, leading to unevenness and increased opacity. In contrast, the GEH group exhibited the fewest inflammatory cells and the best-preserved corneal architecture, likely due to the anti-inflammatory properties of EGCG.

Conjunctival epithelial evaluations showed a marked decrease in epithelial thickness in the KCS group compared to the Sham group, aligning with findings in other dry eye models in rats [8, 21]. On the contrary, another study on similar mouse models showed conjunctival epithelial hyperplasia at 4 and 8 weeks after surgery, as well as evidence of inflammatory cell infiltration at mucosa layer, submucosa layer and around meibomian glands [31]. Further evaluation is required to see if similar pathological changes will also be noticed on KCS rat models over the long term. The loss of goblet cells was also observed, resembling results in previous studies in mice and human with aqueous tear deficiency [8, 26, 28]. Higher percentage of HA present in artificial tears was found to better preserve goblet cell density than that with lower HA percentage [40], which suggested the results are consistent with the insignificant differences of the I-drop and OPT groups compared to the Sham group. Interestingly, the GEH group, despite its lower hyaluronic acid concentration, showed superior preservation of goblet cell density, possibly due to the protective effects of EGCG.

For treating KCS associated keratopathy, despite the lack of statistically significant differences among treatment groups in certain assessments, the GEH NPs showed superior outcomes and consistently ranked among the strongest treatment options in most aspects evaluated, including reduced corneal irregularity, improved tear film stability, and lower fluorescein scores, indicating better corneal integrity. Additionally, the GEH group displayed the least inflammatory cell infiltration and maintained a healthier epithelial structure, further underscoring its protective effects. Notably, GEH was administered only twice per day while other treatment groups received applications 3 times per day, highlighting its efficacy even with a lower dosing frequency. While the artificial tears demonstrated variable benefits on KCS led keratopathy across different evaluations, GEH NPs exhibited excellent protective effects and therapeutic potential on dry eye associated keratopathy based on this study. Overall, this study highlights the potential of nanotechnology in enhancing treatment for KCS led keratopathy and warrants further investigation into its clinical applications.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors have declared that they have no conflict of interest.
